title: Which file is dirty?
prompt: A tip says one file in this config tree contains the string 'planted credential'. grep -r searches every file under a directory; adding -l makes it print just the FILENAMES that match instead of the matching lines. Find the dirty file and read it — the flag is inside.

files.contents:
interval=60
endpoint=internal
# planted credential — do not commit
token: {{FLAG}}

details.restate: One file, somewhere under this tree, contains the phrase 'planted credential'. Locate it with a recursive search, open it, submit the flag written next to the credential marker.

io.returns: (the path of the one dirty file)
io.because: -r sweeps the tree, -l reports which files matched instead of the lines

help.plain: The boss mission taught grep -r for lines. The -l flag answers a different question: WHICH file.
help.analogy: A drug dog sweep — you don't need the dog to read you the contraband, just to sit next to the right suitcase.
help.mnemonic: grep -l: l for List the files.
help.nudge: You could read every line grep prints... or ask grep to just name the file. Which flag does that?
